reply_content:
Despite Petra Costa's defense that her work is a "revelation" of facts, seeking dialogue instead of "mere propaganda," the result is... predictable. This documentary is as subtle as an elephant in a china shop. It doesn't just distort reality; it does so with the grace of someone who already knows where the story is headed.
It’s almost ironic to see the director, who claims to have such a keen vision of our country, coming from a bubble of privilege. She was educated at top institutions like USP and universities abroad, and lives a life she calls "nomadic," but which for most of us is just the elite living in a parallel reality.
While she shows us the "truth" through her lens, she conveniently leaves out some uncomfortable details. For instance, she ignores that while Brazil was second in absolute deaths during the pandemic (a fact that was conveniently used to hammer home the criticism against the government), its ranking plummets to 15th or 20th place when you look at the rate per million inhabitants. The narrative is so well-crafted that you almost forget to check the numbers.
And, to top it all off, there's the analysis of the gospel. The director shows a grasp of the topic as deep as a tourist who tries to understand Brazil in three days. By ignoring basic tools like the mythological framework of the Bible, she delivers a superficial and limited view, proving it’s much easier to film your own beliefs than to truly understand those of others.
